title: Express the Area of Each Part as a Unit Fraction
essential_question: How can you describe the area of each equal part of a shape using a unit fraction?

pretest.question: Which of these is a UNIT FRACTION?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Unit fractions have 1 as the top number.

feedback.correct: Yes! 1/4 is a unit fraction — it has 1 on top.
feedback.incorrect: A unit fraction has 1 as the numerator. 1/4 fits.

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! 1/4 is a unit fraction — it has 1 on top.

pretest.question: A square is divided into 6 equal parts. The area of each part is __ of the square's area. (Write as 1/n)

6/6.note: That is the whole, not one part

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: 6 equal parts → each is 1/6 of the area.

feedback.correct: Right! Each part has area 1/6 of the whole square.
feedback.incorrect: 6 equal parts means each part is 1/6 of the area.

pretest.feedback_correct: Right! Each part has area 1/6 of the whole square.

pretest.question: Which unit fraction is the LARGEST piece (on the same whole)?

B.note: 1/2 is bigger — fewer parts means bigger pieces

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Bigger denominator = smaller piece.

feedback.correct: Right! 1/2 is the largest unit fraction here — only 2 parts means each is the biggest.
feedback.incorrect: 1/2 is the largest. With only 2 parts, each is the biggest.

pretest.feedback_correct: Right! 1/2 is the largest unit fraction here — only 2 parts means each is the biggest.

pretest.question: If you put 4 pieces of size 1/4 together, what fraction do you get?

1/16.note: Add the numerators, not multiply denominators

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Add the top numbers; bottom stays the same.

feedback.incorrect: Adding 1/4 four times gives 4/4 — the whole shape.

pretest.question: A rectangle is divided into 4 equal parts. The whole rectangle has an area of 12 square cm. What is the area of EACH part?

D.note: 12 is the whole, not one part

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Divide the whole area by the number of equal parts.

feedback.incorrect: Each part is 12 ÷ 4 = 3 square cm.

learn.title: What Is a Unit Fraction?
learn.content: A UNIT FRACTION is a fraction with 1 as the NUMERATOR (top number). Examples: 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/6, 1/8. Unit fractions describe ONE piece of an equally-partitioned whole. The DENOMINATOR (bottom number) tells you how many equal parts the whole is divided into.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Equal Parts Have Equal Areas
learn.content: When a shape is partitioned into n EQUAL parts, every part has the SAME AREA. Example: a rectangle divided into 4 equal parts — each part has the same area, even if the parts look different (one might be a strip, another a square).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Go Math 12.6: equal parts = equal areas.

learn.title: Each Part Is 1/n of the Area
learn.content: If a shape is partitioned into n equal parts, the AREA of each part is 1/n of the whole's area. Examples: 2 equal parts → each has area 1/2 of the whole. 4 equal parts → each has area 1/4. 8 equal parts → each has area 1/8.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Add Unit Fractions to Make a Whole
learn.content: Adding all the equal parts gives back the WHOLE. 1/2 + 1/2 = 2/2 = whole. 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4 = 4/4 = whole. 1/8 + 1/8 + ... eight times = 8/8 = whole. The number of unit fractions needed equals the denominator.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Go Math 12.6: unit fractions sum to 1.

learn.title: Bigger Denominator → Smaller Piece
learn.content: When you compare unit fractions of the SAME WHOLE: a bigger denominator means a smaller piece. Order from biggest to smallest: 1/2 > 1/3 > 1/4 > 1/6 > 1/8. Why? More equal parts means each part is smaller.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Combine Unit Fractions
learn.content: When you put two or more unit fractions of the SAME size together, you get a NON-UNIT fraction. Example: 1/4 + 1/4 = 2/4. 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 = 3/8. The denominator stays the same; you add the numerators.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Find Each Part's Area
learn.content: If you know the area of the WHOLE shape, you can find the area of each equal part. Divide the whole's area by the number of parts. Example: a rectangle with area 24 sq cm divided into 4 equal parts — each part has area 24 ÷ 4 = 6 sq cm.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Go Math 12.6: divide to find part area.

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today you learned: (1) UNIT FRACTION = fraction with 1 as numerator. (2) Each equal part has 1/n of the whole's area. (3) Adding all unit fractions = whole (n/n). (4) Bigger denominator = smaller piece. (5) Find part area: divide whole area by n. (6) Combine unit fractions to make non-unit fractions.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Which is NOT a unit fraction?
solution_steps: Step 1: Unit fractions have 1 on top.
Step 3: 2/5 has 2 on top — NOT a unit fraction.

feedback.correct: Right! 2/5 has 2 on top, so it is NOT a unit fraction.
feedback.incorrect: 2/5 is not a unit fraction. Unit fractions must have 1 on top.

feedback.correct: Right! Each part is 1/8 of the circle.

6.note: 6 is the number of parts, not part area

feedback.incorrect: Each part has area 18 ÷ 6 = 3 sq cm.

try.question: How many pieces of 1/3 do you need to make 1 whole?
solution_steps: Step 2: All 3 parts make the whole.

feedback.incorrect: 3 pieces of 1/3 make 1 whole. 3 × 1/3 = 3/3.

verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: The denominator tells you how many pieces make the whole.

try.question: Which area is BIGGER on the same whole?
solution_steps: Step 3: More parts = smaller area each.

feedback.correct: Right! 1/3 covers more area than 1/8 on the same whole.
feedback.incorrect: 1/3 is bigger. Smaller denominator means bigger area on the same whole.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area

practice_r3.question: Anna has a same-size brownie as Tom. Anna eats 2 pieces of 1/8. Tom eats 1 piece of 1/4. Who ate MORE?

feedback.correct: Right! Both ate 1/4 (Anna ate 2/8 which equals 1/4).
feedback.incorrect: They ate the same amount. 2/8 = 1/4 = what Tom ate.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! Both ate 1/4 (Anna ate 2/8 which equals 1/4).

practice_r3.question: A rectangle is divided into 6 equal parts. The whole rectangle has area 30 sq cm. What is the TOTAL area of 4 of these parts? (Write the number.)
solution_steps: Step 1: 30 sq cm ÷ 6 parts = 5 sq cm per part.
Step 2: 4 parts × 5 sq cm = 20 sq cm.

feedback.correct: Right! Each part = 30 ÷ 6 = 5. 4 parts = 4 × 5 = 20 sq cm.
feedback.incorrect: Each part is 5 sq cm. 4 parts = 4 × 5 = 20 sq cm.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: Whole area? Read 2: Each part's area? Read 3: 4 parts together?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L6_unit_fractions_area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: First find one part's area, then multiply by 4.
practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! Each part = 30 ÷ 6 = 5. 4 parts = 4 × 5 = 20 sq cm.
